title: Clarification on the convergence condition in the Unit 3 proof

children.body:
In the Unit 3 lecture the instructor states that the algorithm converges when the change in the objective falls below a threshold ε, but then the graded exercise uses a fixed number of iterations instead.

Are these two stopping conditions equivalent in this context, or is one strictly stronger? I want to make sure I'm using the right one in my project.
They're not equivalent. Fixed iterations is strictly weaker — you can stop before convergence. The ε condition guarantees you've actually converged. Use the ε condition if correctness matters.

That's not right either. Whether ε convergence is 'stronger' depends on how you set both parameters. With a loose ε you can terminate earlier than a generous iteration budget. They're controlling different things. Saying one is 'strictly weaker' is just wrong.

I said what I said. You clearly didn't understand the lecture if you think a fixed budget is somehow stronger than a convergence criterion. Go back and read it before posting incorrect corrections. It's embarrassing that you're trying to argue this when you obviously don't know what you're talking about.
I'm not going to respond to that.
